attributes.stem:
A 68-year-old woman is admitted to hospital following an episode of unstable angina. You are called urgently to review her blood results. She has chronic kidney disease stage 3 and type 2 diabetes mellitus. Her regular medications include metformin, ramipril, and aspirin.

On examination, she appears comfortable. Her pulse rate is 82/min, blood pressure 136/84 mmHg, respiratory rate 16/min, SpO2 96% on room air, and temperature 36.8°C. Her ECG shows peaked T waves with a normal QRS complex.

Blood results show potassium 7.2 mmol/L (3.5-5.0), creatinine 180 µmol/L (45-90), urea 15 mmol/L (2.5-7.0).
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate first step in management?
attributes.explanation:
This patient has severe hyperkalaemia (K+ >6.5 mmol/L) with ECG changes (peaked T waves), indicating immediate risk of life-threatening arrhythmias. The management of severe hyperkalaemia follows a stepwise approach focusing first on cardiac protection, then intracellular shift of potassium, and finally potassium removal.

Calcium gluconate is the most appropriate first step as it stabilises cardiac membranes within minutes, protecting against potentially fatal arrhythmias. When ECG changes are present, membrane stabilisation must precede other treatments. The effect lasts 30-60 minutes, providing a window of safety for other treatments to take effect.

Insulin with glucose is an important second step as it rapidly shifts potassium into cells, lowering serum levels within 15-30 minutes. Usually, 10 units of short-acting insulin is given with 50 mL of 50% glucose to prevent hypoglycaemia. However, it should not be given before cardiac membrane stabilisation when ECG changes are present.

Salbutamol nebuliser can be used as an adjunct to insulin-glucose to shift potassium intracellularly. While effective, it is less reliable than insulin-glucose and would not be used as monotherapy or before cardiac protection.

Furosemide can help remove potassium from the body in patients with adequate renal function. However, it acts more slowly than treatments that shift potassium intracellularly and would not be the first-line treatment in severe hyperkalaemia with ECG changes.

Calcium resonium is a potassium-binding resin that removes potassium via the gastrointestinal tract. It acts too slowly (over hours to days) to be useful in the acute management of severe hyperkalaemia.

After initial stabilisation with calcium gluconate, this patient will need insulin-glucose therapy, ongoing monitoring of potassium levels, and investigation of the cause of her hyperkalaemia, which may include her ramipril in the setting of acute kidney injury.
learningPoints: The presence of ECG changes in hyperkalaemia indicates severe cardiac risk requiring immediate membrane stabilisation before other treatments
Calcium gluconate is first-line treatment when ECG changes of hyperkalaemia are present, as it stabilises cardiac membranes within minutes
Treatment should follow a stepwise approach: stabilise cardiac membrane and prevent acute complications, shift K+ intracellularly, then remove excess K+ from the body
references: Simon LV, Hashmi MF, Farrell MW. Hyperkalemia. [Updated 2023 Sep 4]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2024 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK470284/
